title: Custom Joinery vs Flat Pack: Which Is Better for Your Sydney Home?
metaTitle: Custom Joinery vs Flat Pack Sydney — Which Is Actually Better? | InsideOut Joinery
metaDescription: The real difference between custom joinery and flat pack cabinetry for Sydney homes. When each one makes sense, and what you are actually paying for.
heroAlt: Custom joinery kitchen in Sydney — built to exact room dimensions with integrated storage
excerpt: Flat pack is cheaper upfront. Custom joinery lasts longer and fits properly. The right answer depends on what you are renovating and why.

sections.heading: This is not really a debate — it is a decision about what you need
sections.body: The custom joinery versus flat pack question comes up constantly, and the answer is not one-size-fits-all. Flat pack cabinetry has a legitimate place. Custom joinery has a legitimate place. The mistake is treating one as always better than the other rather than thinking clearly about what the job actually requires. The question to ask is not which option is better in general — it is which option is better for this specific renovation, in this specific home, with this specific budget.

sections.heading: What flat pack actually is
sections.body: Flat pack cabinetry — the kind sold at IKEA, Bunnings, and cabinet suppliers — comes in standardised sizes. Cabinets are 600mm wide, 400mm wide, 900mm wide. They are designed to be assembled and installed by anyone with basic tools, or by a flat pack installer. The upfront cost is lower than custom joinery because the components are mass-produced to fixed dimensions. The limitation is that your kitchen, bathroom or wardrobe is probably not the exact same dimensions as the standard cabinet sizes. Gaps get filled with filler strips. Corners get wasted. End panels get bolted on. The result can look fine, but it does not fit the room — it fits itself and the room works around it.

sections.heading: What custom joinery actually is
sections.body: Custom joinery is built to the exact dimensions of your space. Every cabinet is made to the measurements of your room — ceiling height, wall width, service positions, structural constraints. There are no fillers and no wasted corners because the joinery is designed around the space it is going into rather than the other way around. The materials are generally thicker and better quality than flat pack board products. The hardware — hinges, drawer runners, soft-close mechanisms — is specified for the weight and use of the cabinetry rather than being the cheapest option that passes. And the finish options are wider — polyurethane, two-pac, timber veneers, and combinations that flat pack simply cannot offer.

sections.heading: When flat pack makes sense
sections.body: Flat pack works well in investment properties where you need a functional, presentable kitchen at a cost that makes sense given the return. It works for straightforward layouts where the standard sizes happen to suit the room — a simple galley kitchen with standard-height ceilings and no awkward corners. It works as a temporary solution when budget is the overriding constraint and you plan to renovate properly later. And it works when the owner is willing to manage the installation themselves and is comfortable with a result that is functional rather than tailored.

sections.heading: When custom joinery wins
sections.body: Custom joinery is the right choice for your primary home where you are spending significant time every day and where the kitchen or storage genuinely affects how the house functions and feels. It is the right choice for any space with a non-standard layout — apartments with odd dimensions, terraces with asymmetrical walls, older homes where nothing is square or level. It is the right choice when you want storage that solves a real problem rather than just filling a wall. And it is the right choice when you are spending $30,000 or more on a renovation — at that level, the cost difference between custom and flat pack is a small percentage of the total, and the gap in result is significant.

sections.heading: The 3D render difference
sections.body: One of the clearest advantages of working with a custom joinery business rather than a flat pack installer is the design process. We produce a 3D CAD render of every kitchen before anything is ordered or demolished. You see exactly what you are getting — the layout, the storage configuration, the material palette, the proportions of the island and the cabinetry — and you can change it before it becomes expensive. Flat pack does not offer this. You choose from a catalogue and hope the dimensions work. The render step is not just a nice-to-have. It is the most important part of the process because it lets you make decisions with full information rather than guessing.

sections.heading: The honest cost comparison
sections.body: A flat pack kitchen for a standard three-bedroom Sydney home might cost $8,000 to $15,000 supply and install. A custom kitchen for the same home might cost $25,000 to $45,000. That is a real difference and it is not trivial. But the custom kitchen will be built to your exact dimensions, with better materials, with a design process that solves the actual storage problems in your kitchen, and with a finish that is not available in any flat pack range. If you are planning to live in the home for ten or more years, the cost per year of ownership is not that different. If you are renovating to sell or rent, flat pack may be the right call. The answer depends on what you are actually trying to achieve.

ctaTitle: Not sure which direction is right for your project?
ctaText: Send through your plans or photos and we can give you an honest view on what the space needs — whether that is custom joinery, a more straightforward solution, or something in between.
